answer:

answer:
The David Jones House was built in 1874 [RAM0183-D04] (retracted due to counter_evidence).
The house known as the David H. Jones House is located at 143 South Main Street in Spanish Fork, Utah, United States and was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1985; it was built in 1912 for David H. and Mary E. Nielsen Jones, who lived there until their deaths in 1959 and 1976, respectively. In addition to running his own farm and livestock operations, David Jones served as Commissioner of Agriculture for Utah, as president of the Utah State Farm Bureau, and as president of the Utah County Cooperative Dairy for 20 years. His political career included six years of service as a Spanish Fork city councilman.
Jones House was built in either 1912 or 1895 depending on the source; one document states it was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1985 while another mentions a different date, though other records indicate an NRHP listing year of 1989 [RAM0183-D01][RAM0183-D02].

answer:
The creator depends on which work titled "The Wilderness Years" is referenced: it was written by Ferdinand Fairfax (with Martin Gilbert as co-writer) for a 1981 drama serial about Winston Churchill [RAM0308-D06], or by Sue Townsend for the fourth book in the Adrian Mole series published in 1993 [RAM0308-D01]
Additionally, "The Wilderness Years" is an album created by British singer-songwriter Nick Lowe released in 1991 [RAM0308-D04], while a work titled *Winston Churchill: The Wilderness Years*—which corresponds to the latter half of volume five of Martin Gilbert's biography covering the decade from 1929 to 1939 [RAM0308-D08] and is also an eight-part drama serial based on that period with Gilbert as co-writer [RAM0308-D06]—is a separate entity.

The act enacted on April 7, **1848**, is also mentioned as a related statute (though the text notes it was part of a movement away from common law traditions)

answer:
The feast day for Saint Marcellus varies depending on which specific saint is referenced in the text: while one source identifies a third- or fourth-century martyr of Capua with a feast originally listed as 7 October (later moved to 15 January and then reassigned to 15 December), another venerated centurion from Tangier celebrated his feast on October 30, and yet other historical authorities record a Marcellus associated with the papacy whose feast was observed on 16 January.
*   **Saint Marcellus of Capua:** His feast day is listed as **15 January** [RAM0470-D04] and also **7 October** (noting that Apuleius was removed from this date) [RAM0470-D03]
Marcellus of Tangier (Saint Marcellus the Centurion): His feast day is celebrated on October 30 [RAM0470-D06], though this may refer to a different saint than Marcellus of Capua, who has his own distinct feast date and historical context.
**Pope Saint Marcellus I:** While one text notes that Apuleius was moved to 8 October following changes in 1716 when this day became the feast of Our Lady of the Rosary, another source states a commemoration date for Pope Mark on 7 October; however, evidence indicates that while Marcellus (likely confused with or conflated with other figures such as Marcellus of Capua) was historically commemorated around late September/early October in some traditions before being reassigned to 15 December in 1969 and Apuleius expunged from the official list due to lack of historical foundation, Pope Saint Marcellus I's papacy ended in January.
Another entry mentions that according to certain authorities such as the "Depositio episcoporum" of the Chronography of 354, Marcellus died shortly after leaving Rome with a feast day of **16 January**, though this is contrasted by other sources citing his feast on 7 October or 15 January.
but this refers to Pope Marcellus I's death/papacy end rather than the general feast in all calendars [RAM0470-D07]
Therefore:
*   Saint Marcellus of Capua is celebrated on 15 January or 7 October
Saint Marcellus of Tangier is celebrated on October 30; however, this date may be confused with that of Saint Marcellus of Capua (c. third or fourth century), who was inserted into the General Roman Calendar in the 13th century and has a feast day listed as either July 7 or January 15 depending on the calendar edition, though historical foundations for some associated legends are considered untrustworthy.

answer:
The Benevolent and Protective Order of Elks is an American organization founded in 1868; however, this specific founding date applies to the US entity which shares a common history with but is distinct from other organizations such as the IBPOEW (founded 1897) and the Elks of Canada (founded 1912).
The Improved Benevolent and Protective Order of Elks of the World is an African-American fraternal order modeled on the BPOE that was established in 1897 [RAM0499-D04], while the Elks of Canada were founded in Vancouver, British Columbia by three men from Seattle who appeared to be motivated primarily by financial gain before early applicants took control and began building the organization earnestly; although one source states it was founded in 1912 [RAM0499-D01] (with a Royal Purple auxiliary incorporated under a special Dominion of Canada Charter on September 11, 1914) [RAM0499-D07], another indicates the organization began with actors and entertainers led by Charles Algernon Sidney Vivian who chose the name "Elks" in 1868 prior to its official founding date.

The Improved Benevolent and Protective Order of Elks of the World, a separate African-American fraternal order modeled on the BPOE, was established in 1897 [RAM0499-D04], while the Elks of Canada were founded in 1912 [RAM0499-D01]
